1:
"Observe the month of Abib, and keep the passover unto the LORD thy God: for in the month of Abib the LORD thy God brought thee forth out of Egypt by night." The witnesses note the keeping of the Passover in Abib, the month of the Exodus by night. The witnesses note that Gill notes Abib, sometimes called Nisan, answered to part of March and April, named from the corn appearing in ear; it was appointed the first of the months. The witnesses note that in this month, by night, the LORD brought them out of Egypt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the observe Abib — the month of the Exodus; the keep the passover — the feast of redemption; the brought thee out by night — the night of deliverance; the Christ our passover — the feast fulfilled in the Lamb (1 Cor. 5:7).

The witnesses agree the Passover bound the feast to the memory of redemption: kept in Abib, the month the LORD brought them out of Egypt by night, it was a yearly remembrance of their deliverance; the careful fixing of the time roots the feast in the historic act of salvation, that the people might never forget the night of their freeing — and the whole points to the greater Passover, "Christ our passover... sacrificed for us" (1 Cor. 5:7), the feast of redemption fulfilled in the Lamb whose death delivers his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the Passover in Abib: The witnesses note the observe Abib: the month of the Exodus. The witnesses note the keep the passover: the feast of redemption. The witnesses note the brought thee out by night: the night of deliverance. The witnesses note the Christ our passover: the feast fulfilled in the Lamb (1 Cor. 5:7). The expositors observe the observe Abib: the month of the Exodus. The expositors observe the keep the passover: the feast of redemption. The expositors observe the brought thee out by night: the night of deliverance. The expositors observe the Christ our passover: the feast fulfilled in the Lamb (1 Cor. 5:7). The believer is taught to mark the observe Abib, the keep the passover, the brought thee out by night, the Christ our passover, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the Passover was kept in Abib, the month of the Exodus by night — the yearly remembrance of redemption, fulfilled in Christ our passover sacrificed for us (1 Cor. 5:7).
2:
"Thou shalt therefore sacrifice the passover unto the LORD thy God, of the flock and the herd, in the place which the LORD shall choose to place his name there." The witnesses note the Passover sacrificed to the LORD at the chosen place — the lamb typical of Christ. The witnesses note that Gill notes the passover, a lamb killed on the fourteenth at night between the two evenings, was typical of Christ, the passover sacrificed for us. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the sacrifice at the chosen place. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sacrifice the passover — the lamb slain; the of the flock — the appointed victim; the at the chosen place — the one place of sacrifice; the typical of Christ — the Lamb of God (John 1:29).

The witnesses agree the Passover lamb was the clearest of the types, slain at the chosen place: the lamb killed on the fourteenth at evening, its blood the token of deliverance, plainly figured Christ; Gill draws the line directly — the passover "typical of Christ, the passover sacrificed for us"; the gathering of all to the one chosen place to slay it foreshadowed the one sacrifice, offered once for all, by whose blood the people of God are passed over and redeemed (cf. John 1:29, the Lamb of God).

So the witnesses make the verse the passover sacrificed: The witnesses note the sacrifice the passover: the lamb slain. The witnesses note the of the flock: the appointed victim. The witnesses note the at the chosen place: the one place of sacrifice. The witnesses note the typical of Christ: the Lamb of God (John 1:29). The expositors observe the sacrifice the passover: the lamb slain. The expositors observe the of the flock: the appointed victim. The expositors observe the at the chosen place: the one place of sacrifice. The expositors observe the typical of Christ: the Lamb of God (John 1:29). The believer is taught to mark the sacrifice the passover, the of the flock, the at the chosen place, the typical of Christ, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the Passover was sacrificed at the chosen place — the lamb typical of Christ, the one sacrifice by whose blood God's people are passed over and redeemed (John 1:29).
3:
"Thou shalt eat no leavened bread with it; seven days shalt thou eat unleavened bread therewith, even the bread of affliction... that thou mayest remember the day when thou camest forth out of Egypt..." The witnesses note the unleavened "bread of affliction" eaten with the Passover, to keep the memory of the Exodus. The witnesses note that Gill notes no leavened bread was to be eaten with the passover. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the unleavened "bread of affliction" was unsavoury, putting them in mind of their afflictive bondage, and of the haste of their departure. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no leavened bread — put away with the passover; the the bread of affliction — recalling the bondage; the that thou mayest remember — the day of the Exodus; the purge the old leaven — the redeemed cleansed of sin (1 Cor. 5:8).

The witnesses agree the unleavened bread was a memorial of both affliction and haste: eaten with the Passover for seven days, the "bread of affliction" recalled the bitterness of bondage and the hurried flight from Egypt, keeping the memory of redemption fresh; the putting-away of leaven the apostle reads as the gospel call to purity — "purge out therefore the old leaven... keep the feast... with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth" (1 Cor. 5:8) — the redeemed remembering their deliverance by a life cleansed of the leaven of sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the bread of affliction: The witnesses note the no leavened bread: put away with the passover. The witnesses note the the bread of affliction: recalling the bondage. The witnesses note the that thou mayest remember: the day of the Exodus. The witnesses note the purge the old leaven: the redeemed cleansed of sin (1 Cor. 5:8). The expositors observe the no leavened bread: put away with the passover. The expositors observe the the bread of affliction: recalling the bondage. The expositors observe the that thou mayest remember: the day of the Exodus. The expositors observe the purge the old leaven: the redeemed cleansed of sin (1 Cor. 5:8). The believer is taught to mark the no leavened bread, the the bread of affliction, the that thou mayest remember, the purge the old leaven, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the unleavened bread of affliction was eaten to remember the Exodus — the memorial of bondage and haste, the putting-away of leaven the gospel call to a life cleansed of sin (1 Cor. 5:8).
4:
"And there shall be no leavened bread seen with thee in all thy coasts seven days..." The witnesses note the thorough removal of all leaven from their borders for the seven days. The witnesses note that Gill notes that before the passover they were to search diligently every room, hole, and crevice, that no leaven remain anywhere. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the flesh of the passover properly so called. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no leavened bread seen — in all thy coasts; the seven days — the whole feast; the diligent search — every corner cleansed; the purity entire — no hidden corruption left (2 Cor. 7:1).

The witnesses agree the diligence of the search magnifies the lesson of purity: not a particle of leaven was to be "seen" in all their coasts, the houses searched in every corner that none remain; the thoroughness teaches that the putting-away of sin is to be entire, no secret corner of the heart left harbouring the old leaven; the careful, searching cleansing of the house is a figure of the soul's self-examination, that the redeemed keep the feast having purged out every hidden corruption, holiness no half-measure but a clean sweep (cf. 2 Cor. 7:1).

So the witnesses make the verse no leaven seen: The witnesses note the no leavened bread seen: in all thy coasts. The witnesses note the seven days: the whole feast. The witnesses note the diligent search: every corner cleansed. The witnesses note the purity entire: no hidden corruption left (2 Cor. 7:1). The expositors observe the no leavened bread seen: in all thy coasts. The expositors observe the seven days: the whole feast. The expositors observe the diligent search: every corner cleansed. The expositors observe the purity entire: no hidden corruption left (2 Cor. 7:1). The believer is taught to mark the no leavened bread seen, the seven days, the diligent search, the purity entire, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that no leaven was to be seen in all their coasts — the diligent search a figure of the soul's self-examination, the putting-away of sin entire, no hidden corruption left (2 Cor. 7:1).
5:
"Thou mayest not sacrifice the passover within any of thy gates, which the LORD thy God giveth thee:" The witnesses note that the Passover might not be killed in any town, but only at the chosen place. The witnesses note that Gill notes it might not be sacrificed within any of their gates or cities, but only in Jerusalem, the place the Lord chose. The witnesses note that there alone might they kill and eat it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not within thy gates — no offering in the towns; the only the chosen place — where God set his name; the the unity of worship — the nation to one altar; the one way of approach — the many gathered to the one (Acts 4:12).

The witnesses agree the restriction of the Passover to the one chosen place guarded the unity of worship: it could not be offered in any town at will, but only where God set his name, that the whole nation gather to one altar; the rule kept the people's worship from scattering into local and self-chosen rites, binding them to the one appointed place — a figure of the one way of approach to God, that there is "one sacrifice for sins" and one name under heaven by which men must be saved (cf. Acts 4:12), the many gathered to the one.

So the witnesses make the verse not in any gate: The witnesses note the not within thy gates: no offering in the towns. The witnesses note the only the chosen place: where God set his name. The witnesses note the the unity of worship: the nation to one altar. The witnesses note the one way of approach: the many gathered to the one (Acts 4:12). The expositors observe the not within thy gates: no offering in the towns. The expositors observe the only the chosen place: where God set his name. The expositors observe the the unity of worship: the nation to one altar. The expositors observe the one way of approach: the many gathered to the one (Acts 4:12). The believer is taught to mark the not within thy gates, the only the chosen place, the the unity of worship, the one way of approach, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the Passover might be offered only at the chosen place — guarding the unity of worship, the whole nation to one altar, a figure of the one way of approach to God (Acts 4:12).
6:
"But at the place which the LORD thy God shall choose to place his name in, there thou shalt sacrifice the passover at even, at the going down of the sun, at the season that thou camest forth out of Egypt." The witnesses note the Passover offered at the chosen place at evening, the very season of the Exodus. The witnesses note that Gill notes the place where God set his name, the ark and mercy-seat, his Shechinah dwelling — Jerusalem, where Solomon built the temple. The witnesses note that Wesley notes it was offered in the court of the tabernacle or temple. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the place of his name — where God's presence dwelt; the sacrifice at even — at the going down of the sun; the the season of the Exodus — the very hour of deliverance; the a sacred re-enactment — the redemption vividly renewed.

The witnesses agree the time and place together fixed the Passover as a sacred re-enactment: offered "at even, at the going down of the sun," the very hour of the Exodus, and at the place of God's name, the feast set the worshipper in living memory of the night of deliverance; the precise timing made the yearly observance a vivid renewal of the redemption, the people keeping it at the same season and the same chosen place, that the act of salvation be commemorated with exactness and reverence before the God who dwelt among them.

So the witnesses make the verse at even, at the season: The witnesses note the the place of his name: where God's presence dwelt. The witnesses note the sacrifice at even: at the going down of the sun. The witnesses note the the season of the Exodus: the very hour of deliverance. The witnesses note the a sacred re-enactment: the redemption vividly renewed. The expositors observe the the place of his name: where God's presence dwelt. The expositors observe the sacrifice at even: at the going down of the sun. The expositors observe the the season of the Exodus: the very hour of deliverance. The expositors observe the a sacred re-enactment: the redemption vividly renewed. The believer is taught to mark the the place of his name, the sacrifice at even, the the season of the Exodus, the a sacred re-enactment, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the Passover was offered at evening at the chosen place, the season of the Exodus — the feast a vivid re-enactment of the night of deliverance, the redemption renewed before the God who dwelt among them.
7:
"And thou shalt roast and eat it in the place which the LORD thy God shall choose: and thou shalt turn in the morning, and go unto thy tents." The witnesses note the Passover eaten at the chosen place, the people returning to their tents in the morning. The witnesses note that Gill notes the word for "roast" can mean "boil," but the passover lamb was not to be boiled (expressly forbidden), so some take the Chagigah to be meant. The witnesses note that they turned in the morning to their tents. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the roast and eat it — at the chosen place; the not boiled — the lamb roasted with fire; the turn in the morning — the people returning home; the part of the type — the lamb passed through the fire.

The witnesses agree the verse completes the ordinance of the meal: the Passover was roasted (not boiled, the lamb being expressly forbidden to be seethed) and eaten at the chosen place, and in the morning the people returned home; the careful manner of preparing the lamb — roasted whole with fire — was part of the type, the lamb passed through the fire figuring the suffering of Christ, and the orderly keeping and dispersing showing a worship both reverent and regulated, the feast done according to God's exact appointment.

So the witnesses make the verse roast and eat it: The witnesses note the roast and eat it: at the chosen place. The witnesses note the not boiled: the lamb roasted with fire. The witnesses note the turn in the morning: the people returning home. The witnesses note the part of the type: the lamb passed through the fire. The expositors observe the roast and eat it: at the chosen place. The expositors observe the not boiled: the lamb roasted with fire. The expositors observe the turn in the morning: the people returning home. The expositors observe the part of the type: the lamb passed through the fire. The believer is taught to mark the roast and eat it, the not boiled, the turn in the morning, the part of the type, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the Passover was roasted and eaten at the chosen place, the people returning in the morning — the manner part of the type, the lamb passed through fire, the feast kept by God's exact appointment.
8:
"Six days thou shalt eat unleavened bread: and on the seventh day shall be a solemn assembly to the LORD thy God; thou shalt do no work therein." The witnesses note the seven days of unleavened bread, the seventh a solemn assembly with rest from work. The witnesses note that Gill notes elsewhere it is seven days; the seventh was not exempt from unleavened bread, but distinguished for its special service. The witnesses note that the seventh day was a solemn assembly with no work done. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the six days unleavened bread — the feast continued; the the seventh a solemn assembly — set apart to God; the do no work — a sabbath-rest; the redemption in worship — the people assembling before the LORD (Heb. 10:25).

The witnesses agree the feast was crowned with a holy assembly and rest: through the seven days unleavened bread was eaten, and the seventh was set apart as "a solemn assembly," free from work and given to the worship of God; the sabbath-rest closing the feast shows that the remembrance of redemption was to issue in sacred convocation and holy rest, the people gathering before the LORD to end the feast in worship — a pattern of how the memory of God's deliverance leads his people to assemble and to rest in him (cf. Heb. 10:25).

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn assembly: The witnesses note the six days unleavened bread: the feast continued. The witnesses note the the seventh a solemn assembly: set apart to God. The witnesses note the do no work: a sabbath-rest. The witnesses note the redemption in worship: the people assembling before the LORD (Heb. 10:25). The expositors observe the six days unleavened bread: the feast continued. The expositors observe the the seventh a solemn assembly: set apart to God. The expositors observe the do no work: a sabbath-rest. The expositors observe the redemption in worship: the people assembling before the LORD (Heb. 10:25). The believer is taught to mark the six days unleavened bread, the the seventh a solemn assembly, the do no work, the redemption in worship, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the seventh day was a solemn assembly with rest — the feast crowned with holy convocation, the memory of redemption issuing in worship and rest before the LORD (Heb. 10:25).
9:
"Seven weeks shalt thou number unto thee: begin to number the seven weeks from such time as thou beginnest to put the sickle to the corn." The witnesses note the counting of seven weeks from the harvest to the feast of weeks. The witnesses note that Gill notes that after this counting came the feast of weeks, called Pentecost from the fiftieth day. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the Passover fell when the harvests were beginning to ripen, the firstfruits then offered. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seven weeks numbered — from the harvest; the the sickle to the corn — the firstfruits time; the the feast of weeks — pentecost, the fiftieth day; the fulfilled at Pentecost — the Spirit poured out (Acts 2:1).

The witnesses agree the feast of weeks was bound to the harvest by a counted interval: seven weeks were numbered from the putting of the sickle to the corn, leading to the fiftieth day, Pentecost; the counting tied the feast to the firstfruits of the grain, a thanksgiving for the harvest God gave; and the day was richly fulfilled in the gospel, for on this very Pentecost the Spirit was poured out, the firstfruits of the great harvest of souls gathered into the church (cf. Acts 2:1), the feast of firstfruits become the birthday of the Spirit's ingathering.

So the witnesses make the verse number seven weeks: The witnesses note the seven weeks numbered: from the harvest. The witnesses note the the sickle to the corn: the firstfruits time. The witnesses note the the feast of weeks: Pentecost, the fiftieth day. The witnesses note the fulfilled at Pentecost: the Spirit poured out (Acts 2:1). The expositors observe the seven weeks numbered: from the harvest. The expositors observe the the sickle to the corn: the firstfruits time. The expositors observe the the feast of weeks: Pentecost, the fiftieth day. The expositors observe the fulfilled at Pentecost: the Spirit poured out (Acts 2:1). The believer is taught to mark the seven weeks numbered, the the sickle to the corn, the the feast of weeks, the fulfilled at Pentecost, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that seven weeks were numbered from the harvest to the feast of weeks — the thanksgiving for firstfruits, fulfilled at Pentecost when the Spirit was poured out and the firstfruits of souls gathered (Acts 2:1).
10:
"And thou shalt keep the feast of weeks unto the LORD thy God with a tribute of a freewill offering of thine hand, which thou shalt give unto the LORD..." The witnesses note the feast of weeks kept with a freewill offering measured by God's blessing. The witnesses note that Gill notes the feast of Pentecost, at which the Spirit was poured down on the apostles. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the tribute of a freewill offering of the hand, given as the LORD had blessed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep the feast of weeks — pentecost unto the LORD; the a freewill offering — a glad, free gift; the of thine hand — measured by his blessing; the giving as prospered — free and cheerful (1 Cor. 16:2).

The witnesses agree the feast of weeks was kept with a free and proportioned gift: a "freewill offering" measured by what God had given, the worshipper rendering of his harvest as his blessing allowed; the willingness of the gift marks the spirit of true worship — not a fixed tax wrung from duty but a glad offering flowing from gratitude, proportioned to the bounty received; the principle abides for the church, that giving to God is to be free, cheerful, and according as he hath prospered us (cf. 1 Cor. 16:2), the heart's thankfulness measuring the hand's gift.

So the witnesses make the verse a freewill offering: The witnesses note the keep the feast of weeks: Pentecost unto the LORD. The witnesses note the a freewill offering: a glad, free gift. The witnesses note the of thine hand: measured by his blessing. The witnesses note the giving as prospered: free and cheerful (1 Cor. 16:2). The expositors observe the keep the feast of weeks: Pentecost unto the LORD. The expositors observe the a freewill offering: a glad, free gift. The expositors observe the of thine hand: measured by his blessing. The expositors observe the giving as prospered: free and cheerful (1 Cor. 16:2). The believer is taught to mark the keep the feast of weeks, the a freewill offering, the of thine hand, the giving as prospered, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the feast of weeks was kept with a freewill offering measured by God's blessing — true worship a glad, free gift, giving to God according as he hath prospered us (1 Cor. 16:2).
11:
"And thou shalt rejoice before the LORD thy God, thou, and thy son, and thy daughter... and the Levite... and the stranger, and the fatherless, and the widow, that are among you..." The witnesses note the festal joy shared by all — household, Levite, stranger, fatherless, and widow. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to make a liberal feast and keep it cheerfully before God, thankfully acknowledging his mercies. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the offerings were eaten with festivity and shared with friends in liberality. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rejoice before the LORD — a cheerful feast; the thy whole household — the family glad together; the Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow — the needy drawn in; the joy and charity wed — the table widened to the poor (Neh. 8:10).

The witnesses agree the feast of weeks was a feast of inclusive joy: the worshipper was to "rejoice before the LORD" with his whole household and to draw in the Levite, stranger, fatherless, and widow, the gladness shared with all and especially the needy; the law thus weds worship, joy, and charity, the thankful feast made a means of kindness to the poor; true rejoicing before God overflows in generosity, the festal table widened to include those who have little, that none be left out of the common gladness (cf. Neh. 8:10, send portions unto them for whom nothing is prepared).

So the witnesses make the verse rejoice with all: The witnesses note the rejoice before the LORD: a cheerful feast. The witnesses note the thy whole household: the family glad together. The witnesses note the Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow: the needy drawn in. The witnesses note the joy and charity wed: the table widened to the poor (Neh. 8:10). The expositors observe the rejoice before the LORD: a cheerful feast. The expositors observe the thy whole household: the family glad together. The expositors observe the Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow: the needy drawn in. The expositors observe the joy and charity wed: the table widened to the poor (Neh. 8:10). The believer is taught to mark the rejoice before the LORD, the thy whole household, the Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow, the joy and charity wed, and to rejoice before the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that they were to rejoice before the LORD with household and the needy alike — worship, joy, and charity wed, the festal gladness widened to include the poor, that none be left out (Neh. 8:10).
12:
"And thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in Egypt: and thou shalt observe and do these statutes." The witnesses note the call to remember their bondage, the spring of liberality and compassion in keeping the feast. The witnesses note that Gill notes the remembrance of their deliverance from bondage should make them liberal in their freewill offering, generous in the feast, and compassionate to the stranger, widow, and fatherless. The witnesses note that they were to observe and do these statutes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thou wast a bondman — in Egypt; the remember — the spring of compassion; the liberal and kind — generous to the needy; the observe these statutes — the redeemed made tender (2 Cor. 8:9).

The witnesses agree the memory of bondage was meant to soften and open the heart: remembering that they had been slaves in Egypt and were freed by God, the people were to be liberal in their offerings and kind to the needy at the feast; the recollection of their own past misery and present mercy was the motive of compassion, that those who had been poor and oppressed should pity and provide for the poor and oppressed among them — the redeemed, mindful of their redemption, made generous and tender (cf. 2 Cor. 8:9).

So the witnesses make the verse remember thy bondage: The witnesses note the thou wast a bondman: in Egypt. The witnesses note the remember: the spring of compassion. The witnesses note the liberal and kind: generous to the needy. The witnesses note the observe these statutes: the redeemed made tender (2 Cor. 8:9). The expositors observe the thou wast a bondman: in Egypt. The expositors observe the remember: the spring of compassion. The expositors observe the liberal and kind: generous to the needy. The expositors observe the observe these statutes: the redeemed made tender (2 Cor. 8:9). The believer is taught to mark the thou wast a bondman, the remember, the liberal and kind, the observe these statutes, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that they were to remember their bondage and keep the statutes — the memory of redemption the spring of liberality and compassion, the redeemed made generous and tender to the needy (2 Cor. 8:9).
13:
"Thou shalt observe the feast of tabernacles seven days, after that thou hast gathered in thy corn and thy wine:" The witnesses note the feast of tabernacles kept seven days after the ingathering of corn and wine. The witnesses note that Gill notes it began on the fifteenth of Tisri (September). The witnesses note that Calvin notes its first day was called the day of ingathering, when the produce of the whole year was stored in their granaries. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seven days — the feast of booths; the after the ingathering — corn and wine stored; the the wilderness recalled — dwelling in booths; the the great ingathering — god gathering his harvest (Rev. 7:9).

The witnesses agree the feast of tabernacles crowned the year with thanksgiving for the harvest: kept seven days after the corn and wine were gathered in, it was the feast of ingathering, when the whole year's produce was stored; the dwelling in booths recalled the wilderness wandering, joining the memory of God's past care to thanksgiving for present plenty, so that in the time of fullest harvest the people remembered their pilgrim days — and the feast looked on to the great ingathering, when God shall gather his harvest of the redeemed and they shall dwell with him (cf. Rev. 7:9).

So the witnesses make the verse the feast of tabernacles: The witnesses note the seven days: the feast of booths. The witnesses note the after the ingathering: corn and wine stored. The witnesses note the the wilderness recalled: dwelling in booths. The witnesses note the the great ingathering: God gathering his harvest (Rev. 7:9). The expositors observe the seven days: the feast of booths. The expositors observe the after the ingathering: corn and wine stored. The expositors observe the the wilderness recalled: dwelling in booths. The expositors observe the the great ingathering: God gathering his harvest (Rev. 7:9). The believer is taught to mark the seven days, the after the ingathering, the the wilderness recalled, the the great ingathering, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that the feast of tabernacles was kept after the harvest — thanksgiving for the ingathering joined to the memory of the wilderness, looking on to the great ingathering of the redeemed (Rev. 7:9).
14:
"And thou shalt rejoice in thy feast, thou, and thy son, and thy daughter, and thy manservant... and the Levite, the stranger, and the fatherless, and the widow, that are within thy gates." The witnesses note the festal joy of tabernacles shared by all the household and the needy. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to rejoice at the feast of ingathering in gratitude for God's goodness; the Targum adds, with flute and pipe, using music to increase the joy. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the rejoicing of all, with the needy included. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rejoice in thy feast — gladness for the harvest; the thy whole household — servants and children; the the needy included — levite, stranger, fatherless, widow; the joy widened to all — the community sharing the bounty (Phil. 4:4).

The witnesses agree the harvest feast was a feast of full and shared gladness: the worshipper was to "rejoice" with his whole household, servants, and the needy, the joy heightened even with music; the law again weds thanksgiving and charity, the gladness of plenty made the occasion of kindness to the poor; true festal joy before God is not selfish but widens to embrace all, especially the Levite and the helpless, that the whole community share in the rejoicing over God's bounty (cf. Phil. 4:4).

So the witnesses make the verse rejoice in thy feast: The witnesses note the rejoice in thy feast: gladness for the harvest. The witnesses note the thy whole household: servants and children. The witnesses note the the needy included: Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow. The witnesses note the joy widened to all: the community sharing the bounty (Phil. 4:4). The expositors observe the rejoice in thy feast: gladness for the harvest. The expositors observe the thy whole household: servants and children. The expositors observe the the needy included: Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow. The expositors observe the joy widened to all: the community sharing the bounty (Phil. 4:4). The believer is taught to mark the rejoice in thy feast, the thy whole household, the the needy included, the joy widened to all, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that they were to rejoice in the feast with household and the needy — thanksgiving and charity wed, the festal gladness widened to embrace all, the whole community sharing God's bounty (Phil. 4:4).
15:
"Seven days shalt thou keep a solemn feast unto the LORD thy God in the place which the LORD shall choose: because the LORD thy God shall bless thee in all thine increase... therefore thou shalt surely rejoice." The witnesses note the seven-day feast at the chosen place, the ground of joy being God's blessing on all their increase. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the feast of tabernacles still spoken of, kept seven days unto the LORD. The witnesses note that the blessing of God on their increase and works was the ground of their rejoicing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a solemn feast — seven days at the chosen place; the the LORD shall bless thee — in all thine increase; the therefore rejoice — joy commanded, not optional; the the duty of gladness — thankful joy for his bounty (Deut. 28:47).

The witnesses agree the command makes joy a duty grounded in God's blessing: "because the LORD shall bless thee... therefore thou shalt surely rejoice" — the gladness not optional but commanded, and its reason the goodness of God in their harvest and labour; the joining of blessing and joy teaches that thankful rejoicing is the fitting answer to God's bounty, and that the people of God are bound not only to receive his gifts but to be glad in them before him, a holy obligation of joy laid on those whom he has prospered (cf. Deut. 28:47, serve him with joyfulness for the abundance of all things).

So the witnesses make the verse surely rejoice: The witnesses note the a solemn feast: seven days at the chosen place. The witnesses note the the LORD shall bless thee: in all thine increase. The witnesses note the therefore rejoice: joy commanded, not optional. The witnesses note the the duty of gladness: thankful joy for his bounty (Deut. 28:47). The expositors observe the a solemn feast: seven days at the chosen place. The expositors observe the the LORD shall bless thee: in all thine increase. The expositors observe the therefore rejoice: joy commanded, not optional. The expositors observe the the duty of gladness: thankful joy for his bounty (Deut. 28:47). The believer is taught to mark the a solemn feast, the the LORD shall bless thee, the therefore rejoice, the the duty of gladness, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that the feast was kept seven days because God blessed all their increase, "therefore thou shalt surely rejoice" — joy a commanded duty, thankful gladness the fitting answer to God's bounty (Deut. 28:47).
16:
"Three times in a year shall all thy males appear before the LORD thy God in the place which he shall choose... and they shall not appear before the LORD empty:" The witnesses note the three yearly pilgrim feasts at which all the males were to appear before the LORD, none empty-handed. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is repeated here for the sake of mentioning the place where they were to appear. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the three appearances (Exod. 23:17). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the three times a year — the pilgrim feasts; the all thy males appear — before the LORD; the at the chosen place — the appointed sanctuary; the not empty — each bringing his gift (Ps. 96:8).

The witnesses agree the three yearly appearings bound the nation to regular, costly worship: at Passover, Weeks, and Tabernacles all the males were to "appear before the LORD" at the chosen place, and none "empty," but each bringing his gift; the requirement of a tribute at every appearing teaches that we are not to come before God empty-handed but with an offering, the worshipper acknowledging the Giver by a return of his gifts — a pattern of bringing to God the homage and substance due him, that none approach his presence without a thankful offering (cf. Ps. 96:8, bring an offering, and come into his courts).

So the witnesses make the verse three times a year: The witnesses note the three times a year: the pilgrim feasts. The witnesses note the all thy males appear: before the LORD. The witnesses note the at the chosen place: the appointed sanctuary. The witnesses note the not empty: each bringing his gift (Ps. 96:8). The expositors observe the three times a year: the pilgrim feasts. The expositors observe the all thy males appear: before the LORD. The expositors observe the at the chosen place: the appointed sanctuary. The expositors observe the not empty: each bringing his gift (Ps. 96:8). The believer is taught to mark the three times a year, the all thy males appear, the at the chosen place, the not empty, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that all the males were to appear before the LORD three times a year, none empty — regular, costly worship, each bringing his offering, none approaching God without a thankful gift (Ps. 96:8).
17:
"Every man shall give as he is able, according to the blessing of the LORD thy God which he hath given thee." The witnesses note the measure of the gift — each according to his ability, as God had prospered him. The witnesses note that Gill notes the quantity is not fixed in the law but left to the generosity of the people, the rule being that they give according to their ability as the Lord had prospered them. The witnesses note that every man was to give as he was able. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the give as he is able — proportion, not a fixed sum; the according to the blessing — as God had prospered; the rich more, poor less — equal in proportion; the giving as prospered — the gift measured by the blessing (1 Cor. 16:2).

The witnesses agree the measure of the offering was proportion, not a fixed sum: each was to give "as he is able, according to the blessing of the LORD," the rich more and the poor less, all in keeping with what God had given them; the rule honours both the duty of all to give and the difference of their means, requiring not equal amounts but equal proportion to ability; it abides as the pattern of Christian giving, that we lay by "as God hath prospered" us (1 Cor. 16:2), the gift measured by the blessing received.

So the witnesses make the verse as he is able: The witnesses note the give as he is able: proportion, not a fixed sum. The witnesses note the according to the blessing: as God had prospered. The witnesses note the rich more, poor less: equal in proportion. The witnesses note the giving as prospered: the gift measured by the blessing (1 Cor. 16:2). The expositors observe the give as he is able: proportion, not a fixed sum. The expositors observe the according to the blessing: as God had prospered. The expositors observe the rich more, poor less: equal in proportion. The expositors observe the giving as prospered: the gift measured by the blessing (1 Cor. 16:2). The believer is taught to mark the give as he is able, the according to the blessing, the rich more, poor less, the giving as prospered, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that each was to give as he was able, according to God's blessing — proportion not a fixed sum, the gift measured by the means God gave, the pattern of giving as he hath prospered us (1 Cor. 16:2).
18:
"Judges and officers shalt thou make thee in all thy gates... and they shall judge the people with just judgment." The witnesses note the appointing of judges and officers in every city, to judge the people justly. The witnesses note that Gill notes judges were fixed in the courts of judicature, and officers were the executors who stood before them, ordering weights and measures and punishing wrongdoers. The witnesses note that they were to judge the people with just judgment. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the judges and officers — in all thy gates; the to judge the people — righting wrong; the just judgment — equity in the common life; the rulers God's ministers — set for good and justice (Rom. 13:4).

The witnesses agree the appointing of judges marks God's care for justice in the common life: every city was to have its judges and officers, that right be done and wrong restrained among the people; the provision shows that God wills not only true worship but righteous government, the ordering of society by just judgment being part of his law; rulers are his ministers for good, set to execute justice and maintain order, and the people are to provide and submit to such authority, that the land be governed in equity (cf. Rom. 13:4).

So the witnesses make the verse judges in the gates: The witnesses note the judges and officers: in all thy gates. The witnesses note the to judge the people: righting wrong. The witnesses note the just judgment: equity in the common life. The witnesses note the rulers God's ministers: set for good and justice (Rom. 13:4). The expositors observe the judges and officers: in all thy gates. The expositors observe the to judge the people: righting wrong. The expositors observe the just judgment: equity in the common life. The expositors observe the rulers God's ministers: set for good and justice (Rom. 13:4). The believer is taught to mark the judges and officers, the to judge the people, the just judgment, the rulers God's ministers, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that judges and officers were appointed in every city to judge justly — God's care for righteous government, rulers his ministers set to maintain justice and order among the people (Rom. 13:4).
19:
"Thou shalt not wrest judgment; thou shalt not respect persons, neither take a gift: for a gift doth blind the eyes of the wise, and pervert the words of the righteous." The witnesses note the charge to the judges — pervert not judgment, show no partiality, take no bribe. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is said to the judges as a direction — not to wrest or pervert judgment, nor pass a wrong sentence. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the threefold charge — not to wrest judgment, respect persons, or take a gift, for a gift blinds the wise. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wrest not judgment — no perverting of right; the respect not persons — no partiality; the take no gift — no bribe; the a gift blinds the wise — corruption perverting the discerning (2 Chron. 19:7).

The witnesses agree the charge guards the fountain of justice from its three great corrupters: the judge was not to wrest judgment, nor favour persons, nor take a bribe — "for a gift doth blind the eyes of the wise"; the warning lays bare how partiality and corruption pervert even the discerning, the bribe blinding the clearest sight; the law thus demands an incorruptible justice, free of fear and favour and gain, a standing rebuke to every bending of right for money or persons, that judgment flow pure as becomes the throne of the God who takes no reward (cf. 2 Chron. 19:7).

So the witnesses make the verse no bribe, no partiality: The witnesses note the wrest not judgment: no perverting of right. The witnesses note the respect not persons: no partiality. The witnesses note the take no gift: no bribe. The witnesses note the a gift blinds the wise: corruption perverting the discerning (2 Chron. 19:7). The expositors observe the wrest not judgment: no perverting of right. The expositors observe the respect not persons: no partiality. The expositors observe the take no gift: no bribe. The expositors observe the a gift blinds the wise: corruption perverting the discerning (2 Chron. 19:7). The believer is taught to mark the wrest not judgment, the respect not persons, the take no gift, the a gift blinds the wise, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that the judges were not to wrest judgment, favour persons, or take a bribe, for a gift blinds the wise — the demand for an incorruptible justice, free of fear, favour, and gain (2 Chron. 19:7).
20:
"That which is altogether just shalt thou follow, that thou mayest live, and inherit the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee." The witnesses note the emphatic charge to follow strict justice — "justice, justice" — that they may live and inherit. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Hebrew is "justice, justice" — strict justice, and nothing else. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the emphatic repetition inculcates that judges should study equity with inflexible constancy, nothing being more likely to swerve them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the altogether just — strict justice and nothing else; the the doubled charge — justice, justice; the inflexible constancy — equity unswayed; the the way of life — righteousness the people's welfare (Prov. 21:3).

The reformed reader (Calvin) marks the force of the doubled word: "justice, justice" is no idle repetition but an emphatic charge that judges pursue equity with inflexible constancy, knowing how easily the heart is swayed from right; the pressing of strict and only justice, joined to the promise of life and inheritance, shows that righteousness is the foundation of a people's welfare, the doing of justice the way of national life and blessing — so that to follow what is "altogether just," without bending, is both the duty and the safety of God's people (cf. Prov. 21:3).

So the witnesses make the verse justice, justice: The witnesses note the altogether just: strict justice and nothing else. The witnesses note the the doubled charge: justice, justice. The witnesses note the inflexible constancy: equity unswayed. The witnesses note the the way of life: righteousness the people's welfare (Prov. 21:3). The expositors observe the altogether just: strict justice and nothing else. The expositors observe the the doubled charge: justice, justice. The expositors observe the inflexible constancy: equity unswayed. The expositors observe the the way of life: righteousness the people's welfare (Prov. 21:3). The believer is taught to mark the altogether just, the the doubled charge, the inflexible constancy, the the way of life, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that they were to follow strict justice — "justice, justice" — that they might live and inherit — the emphatic charge to inflexible equity, righteousness the foundation of a people's welfare (Prov. 21:3).
21:
"Thou shalt not plant thee a grove of any trees near unto the altar of the LORD thy God, which thou shalt make thee." The witnesses note the ban on planting a grove by the altar — guarding against idolatrous worship. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was lawful to plant trees and groves, but not for a religious or idolatrous use. The witnesses note that Clarke notes groves were planted about idol-temples for idolatrous purposes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the plant no grove — near the altar; the not for idolatry — though trees were lawful; the the heathen manner — groves about idol-shrines; the worship kept pure — shunning the apparatus of idolatry (1 Thess. 5:22).

The witnesses agree the prohibition guards the purity of God's worship from the trappings of idolatry: trees might be planted for use, but not a "grove" by the altar, for the heathen planted such groves about their idol-shrines; the ban kept God's worship clear of every borrowed form of false religion, that nothing of the idolaters' manner cleave to the service of the true God; the principle abides, that the worship of God is to be kept pure of all that belongs to false worship, his people shunning even the appearance and apparatus of idolatry (cf. 1 Thess. 5:22, abstain from all appearance of evil).

So the witnesses make the verse no grove by the altar: The witnesses note the plant no grove: near the altar. The witnesses note the not for idolatry: though trees were lawful. The witnesses note the the heathen manner: groves about idol-shrines. The witnesses note the worship kept pure: shunning the apparatus of idolatry (1 Thess. 5:22). The expositors observe the plant no grove: near the altar. The expositors observe the not for idolatry: though trees were lawful. The expositors observe the the heathen manner: groves about idol-shrines. The expositors observe the worship kept pure: shunning the apparatus of idolatry (1 Thess. 5:22). The believer is taught to mark the plant no grove, the not for idolatry, the the heathen manner, the worship kept pure, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that no grove was to be planted by the altar — God's worship kept clear of every borrowed form of idolatry, his people shunning even the appearance and apparatus of false religion (1 Thess. 5:22).
22:
"Neither shalt thou set thee up any image; which the LORD thy God hateth." The witnesses note the ban on setting up any idolatrous pillar or image, which the LORD hates. The witnesses note that Gill notes the word signifies a statue or pillar set up for idolatry; what was not for idolatry, as a memorial of an event, was not forbidden; the LORD hates every kind of idolatry. The witnesses note that the LORD thy God hates it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the set up no image — no idolatrous pillar; the for worship — not a mere memorial; the which the LORD hateth — god's own abhorrence; the hate what he hates — keeping worship free of idols (Jer. 44:4).

The witnesses agree the closing prohibition sets God's own hatred against idolatry: no image or pillar was to be set up for worship, "which the LORD thy God hateth"; the distinction Gill notes — a memorial-pillar was lawful, an idolatrous one forbidden — keeps the ban precisely on false worship, and the ground given is the strongest possible, that God himself abhors it; the verse teaches that idolatry is not merely forbidden but hateful to God, an abomination he loathes, so that his people must hate what he hates and keep their worship free of every idol (cf. Jer. 44:4, this abominable thing that I hate).

So the witnesses make the verse the image God hates: The witnesses note the set up no image: no idolatrous pillar. The witnesses note the for worship: not a mere memorial. The witnesses note the which the LORD hateth: God's own abhorrence. The witnesses note the hate what he hates: keeping worship free of idols (Jer. 44:4). The expositors observe the set up no image: no idolatrous pillar. The expositors observe the for worship: not a mere memorial. The expositors observe the which the LORD hateth: God's own abhorrence. The expositors observe the hate what he hates: keeping worship free of idols (Jer. 44:4). The believer is taught to mark the set up no image, the for worship, the which the LORD hateth, the hate what he hates, and to follow that which is altogether just. The verse thus records that no idolatrous image was to be set up, "which the LORD thy God hateth" — idolatry not merely forbidden but hateful to God, his people to hate what he hates and keep their worship pure (Jer. 44:4).
